description: In the mid-2000s, Brazil faced a public health controversy over the reintroduction of thalidomide to treat leprosy-related complications. Despite its history of causing severe birth defects, the Brazilian government and ANVISA argued its clinical efficacy for erythema nodosum leprosum outweighed risks, leading to a fierce debate between patient advocacy groups and reproductive health advocates.

key_events.description: ANVISA publishes draft guidelines on strict distribution controls.
Human rights groups file a formal protest citing historical birth defects.
Leprosy patient testimonials highlight the drug as a literal lifeline.
Government implements a mandatory pregnancy test registry for female patients.

calibration_notes: Tests the intersection of past medical trauma and present-day clinical necessity in a developing country context.
